title: Interlayer electronic coherence links magnetism and superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates
authors: Feiyang Liu, Lixing Chen, Enkang Zhang, Ying-Jie Zhang, Jun Zhao
abstract: The extent to which electronic dimensionality influences magnetism and superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper (RP) nickelates remains unsettled. Here we report high-precision crystallographic-axis-resolved dc transport measurements on high-quality single crystals of bilayer and trilayer RP nickelates. Using a six-terminal geometry, we self-consistently determine the intrinsic in-plane ($ρ_\parallel$) and out-of-plane ($ρ_\bot$) resistivities on the same crystal, while minimizing uncertainties associated with current redistribution in highly anisotropic conductors. We uncover strong intrinsic electronic anisotropy in both bilayer and trilayer nickelates, in contrast to the weak anisotropy inferred from conventional four-probe measurements. Moreover, $ρ_\bot$ exhibits a nonmonotonic temperature dependence, revealing a universal coherent-to-incoherent crossover in interlayer transport. Across the RP nickelate series, the maximum superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$) observed under pressure is inversely correlated with the ambient-pressure resistivity anisotropy, suggesting that stronger interlayer electronic coherence is favorable for superconductivity. In addition, $ρ_\bot$ serves as an exceptionally sensitive and selective probe of magnetic and density-wave orders, exhibiting pronounced anomalies, whereas only weak signatures are observed in $ρ_\parallel$. Our results highlight interlayer coherence as a key organizing parameter that both tracks the relevant magnetic correlations and is closely tied to superconductivity, providing stringent constraints on microscopic theories of high-$T_c$ superconductivity in nickelates.
summary: This paper reveals that Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates possess much higher electronic anisotropy than previously thought. It demonstrates that interlayer electronic coherence acts as a key parameter that links magnetic/density-wave orders to the emergence of superconductivity.
main_problem: The study aims to determine how electronic dimensionality influences magnetism and superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates and to resolve discrepancies in previously reported resistivity anisotropy.
main_result: The researchers discovered much higher intrinsic resistivity anisotropy than previously reported and found that stronger interlayer electronic coherence is positively correlated with higher superconducting transition temperatures.
method: High-precision DC transport measurements were performed using a six-terminal geometry on high-quality single crystals to self-consistently determine in-plane and out-of-plane resistivities.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate single crystals, specifically bilayer (La3Ni2O7) and trilayer (La4Ni3O10, Pr4Ni3O10) structures, characterized by Ni-O layers and multi-orbital (Ni 3d) electronic structures.
key_observables: In-plane resistivity (rho_parallel), out-of_plane resistivity (rho_perp), resistivity anisotropy ratio (gamma_rho), and superconducting transition temperature (Tc).
important_parameters: Temperature (T), resistivity anisotropy ratio, effective penetration depth (zeff), and the coherent-to-incoherent transport crossover.
main_assumptions: The extraction of intrinsic resistivities assumes a mathematical model to account for non-uniform current distribution in highly anisotropic conductors.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the six-terminal measurement setup and current distribution; Fig 2 displays temperature-dependent resistivities for various nickelates; Fig 3 confirms Fermi-liquid behavior at low temperatures; Fig 4 shows the temperature dependence of anisotropy and its correlation with Tc.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dimensionality in nickelates, describes the six-terminal experimental method, presents the discovery of high anisotropy and transport crossovers, and concludes by correlating interlayer coherence with superconductivity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
